tossups.question: William Hazlitt's edition of a poem by this author discusses a musical setting of it by Dr. John Wilson and bemoans the text's "extraordinary corruption," while swapping the word "gods" for "birds" in the first stanza. This poet created a speaker who "with shriller throat shall sing" like "committed linnets" of the "glories of my King." This poet borrowed William Habington's image of "the nunnery of thy chaste breast" in one poem, while another poem by this author claims that "fishes that tipple in the deep" and "birds that wanton in the air...know no such liberty" as its speaker. This author, who wrote a poem that ends, "I could not love thee, Dear, so much, lov'd I not Honour more," declared that "stone walls do not a prison make" in another poem. For 10 points, name this Cavalier poet who wrote "To Althea, from Prison."
tossups.question_sanitized: William Hazlitt's edition of a poem by this author discusses a musical setting of it by Dr. John Wilson and bemoans the text's "extraordinary corruption," while swapping the word "gods" for "birds" in the first stanza. This poet created a speaker who "with shriller throat shall sing" like "committed linnets" of the "glories of my King." This poet borrowed William Habington's image of "the nunnery of thy chaste breast" in one poem, while another poem by this author claims that "fishes that tipple in the deep" and "birds that wanton in the air...know no such liberty" as its speaker. This author, who wrote a poem that ends, "I could not love thee, Dear, so much, lov'd I not Honour more," declared that "stone walls do not a prison make" in another poem. For 10 points, name this Cavalier poet who wrote "To Althea, from Prison."

tossups.question: The protagonist of a novel set in this country spends all his time designing a dragon costume that he wears for Carnival. In a short story set in this country, the title character reveals the phrase “the past is deep” and shows the narrator his mango, coconut, and plum trees. A character from this country writes “the greatest poem in the world” at the speed of one line a month and calls himself (*) B. Wordsworth. In a novel set in this country, the protagonist loses his neighbor’s calf because of a pundit’s advice that he be kept away from trees and water due to his extra finger. That character from this country takes a job as a sign painter, leading him to marry into the Tulsi family. For 10 points, Miguel Street and A House for Mr. Biswas are set in what Caribbean country, the birthplace of V.S. Naipaul?
tossups.answer: (Republic of) Trinidad and Tobago (The first clue refers to The Dragon Can’t Dance by Earl Lovelace.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: (Republic of) Trinidad and Tobago (The first clue refers to The Dragon Can't Dance by Earl Lovelace.)
tossups.question_sanitized: The protagonist of a novel set in this country spends all his time designing a dragon costume that he wears for Carnival. In a short story set in this country, the title character reveals the phrase "the past is deep" and shows the narrator his mango, coconut, and plum trees. A character from this country writes "the greatest poem in the world" at the speed of one line a month and calls himself (*) B. Wordsworth. In a novel set in this country, the protagonist loses his neighbor's calf because of a pundit's advice that he be kept away from trees and water due to his extra finger. That character from this country takes a job as a sign painter, leading him to marry into the Tulsi family. For 10 points, Miguel Street and A House for Mr. Biswas are set in what Caribbean country, the birthplace of V.S. Naipaul?

tossups.question: In one work, this author implores “Ah braid no more that shining hair!” while asking for sex. In another poem, he notes that he loved the addressee for twelve hours but now must be “sated with Varietie”. This man, who penned an appeal to Amarantha and The (+) Scrutinie, wrote “I could not love thee Dear so much/ Lov’d I not Honour more” in apologizing to his lover for going to battle. The first three stanzas by one of his poems declare that “Gods that wanton in the Air”, “Fishes that tipple in the Deep”, and “Enlarg`ed Winds, that curl the Flood” all (*) “Know no such Liberty”. This poet notes that “Minds innocent and quiet take/ that for an hermitage” after stating that “Stone walls do not a prison make”. For ten points, name this Cavalier poet of To Lucasta, Going to the Wars and To Althea, From Prison.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one work, this author implores "Ah braid no more that shining hair!" while asking for sex. In another poem, he notes that he loved the addressee for twelve hours but now must be "sated with Varietie". This man, who penned an appeal to Amarantha and The (+) Scrutinie, wrote "I could not love thee Dear so much/ Lov'd I not Honour more" in apologizing to his lover for going to battle. The first three stanzas by one of his poems declare that "Gods that wanton in the Air", "Fishes that tipple in the Deep", and "Enlarg`ed Winds, that curl the Flood" all (*) "Know no such Liberty". This poet notes that "Minds innocent and quiet take/ that for an hermitage" after stating that "Stone walls do not a prison make". For ten points, name this Cavalier poet of To Lucasta, Going to the Wars and To Althea, From Prison.

tossups.question: This writer addressed a woman whose “sad indifference” “both kills and doth reprieve” in a poem that describes its subject as “an angel here, the devil there.” Another work by this poet praises the “sweetness mercy, majesty and glories” of his (*) King, and mentions that “birds that wanton in the air / Know no such liberty” as the speaker “When Love with unconfined wings / Hovers within my gates.” “Stone walls do not a prison make, nor iron bars a cage” according to - for 10 points - what Cavalier poet’s “To Althea, from Prison?”
tossups.question_sanitized: This writer addressed a woman whose "sad indifference" "both kills and doth reprieve" in a poem that describes its subject as "an angel here, the devil there." Another work by this poet praises the "sweetness mercy, majesty and glories" of his (*) King, and mentions that "birds that wanton in the air / Know no such liberty" as the speaker "When Love with unconfined wings / Hovers within my gates." "Stone walls do not a prison make, nor iron bars a cage" according to - for 10 points - what Cavalier poet's "To Althea, from Prison?"

bonuses.leadin: Terry Eagleton argued this novel’s title character subverts the expectations of her class by choosing to die rather than marry her rapist in a book subtitled Writing, Sexuality, and Class Struggle. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Terry Eagleton argued this novel's title character subverts the expectations of her class by choosing to die rather than marry her rapist in a book subtitled Writing, Sexuality, and Class Struggle. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel whose villain rapes its title character at Mrs. Sinclair’s brothel.

After Clarissa is raped, she expresses her trauma through writing “mad” types of these documents with writing scattered throughout the page. Since Clarissa is an epistolary novel, its narrative is conveyed with these documents.

In an alternate ending written by Lady Echlin, Clarissa’s rapist, who has this surname, never rapes her and is instead guided towards virtue. A Cavalier poet with this surname wrote “To Althea, From Prison.”
parts_sanitized: Name this novel whose villain rapes its title character at Mrs. Sinclair's brothel.

After Clarissa is raped, she expresses her trauma through writing "mad" types of these documents with writing scattered throughout the page. Since Clarissa is an epistolary novel, its narrative is conveyed with these documents.

In an alternate ending written by Lady Echlin, Clarissa's rapist, who has this surname, never rapes her and is instead guided towards virtue. A Cavalier poet with this surname wrote "To Althea, From Prison."
answers_sanitized: Lovelace [accept Robert Lovelace; accept Richard Lovelace]

bonuses.leadin: The speaker calls the title animal the "wise emblem of our politic world" curled in "thine own self" in this author's poem "The Snail." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poet who described another insect getting drunk every night while on a "well-filléd oaten beard" in "The Grasshopper," a poem dedicated to his "Noble Friend" Charles Cotton.

This author wrote, "I see the envious caterpillar sit / On the fair blossom of each growing wit" in a poem commending Richard Lovelace. A poem by this author describes an "industrious bee" dwelling where thoughts become "green…in a green shade."
This Andrew Marvell poem, which describes how "worms shall try / That long-preserved virginity," opens by telling the addressee, "Had we but world enough and time."
answers: Andrew Marvell (the poems are "To His Noble Friend, Richard Lovelace, Upon His Poems" and "The Garden.")
answers_sanitized: Andrew Marvell (the poems are "To His Noble Friend, Richard Lovelace, Upon His Poems" and "The Garden.")

bonuses.leadin: This character’s elaborate schemes include purchasing books to make inhabitants of a brothel seem well-read and renting all the rooms of an inn and filling them with impostors hired to impersonate his relatives. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this archetypical rake whose drugging and imprisoning of a woman leads her to write ten “Mad Papers,” the last of which includes sideways and diagonal text.
Lovelace is the villain of this approximately 1,500-page epistolary novel by Samuel Richardson, which Harold Bloom dubiously claimed to reread every year.

Lovelace carries Clarissa to bed after a maid accidentally performs this action while reading Pandosto. Clarissa insists that Lovelace must have performed this action, as he takes the opportunity to grope her. A description is acceptable.
answers: Clarissa [or Clarissa; or, The History of a Young Lady: Comprehending the Most Important Concerns of Private Life. And Particularly Shewing, the Distresses that May Attend the Misconduct Both of Parents and Children, In Relation to Marriage]

almost starting a fire [accept equivalents; accept answers indicating that she sets some curtains on fire or equivalents; accept answers indicating that she knocks over a candle or lamp or equivalents]

almost starting a fire [accept equivalents; accept answers indicating that she sets some curtains on fire or equivalents; accept answers indicating that she knocks over a candle or lamp or equivalents]

bonuses.leadin: This poet wrote that “I could not love thee, dear, so much, / Lov’d I not Honour more” in a poem explaining his decision to fight for Charles I. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Cavalier poet who fought under George Goring in the Bishops’ Wars, which inspired him to write “To Lucasta, Going to the Warres.”
While under Goring, Lovelace wrote another poem dedicated to Lucasta titled for one of these items. Robert Burns wrote a poem about a “red, red” one of these items.

The Bishops’ Wars also inspired Lovelace to write a sonnet to Goring about one of these events. Another poem written for one of these events repeats the line “Sweet Thames, run softly, till I end my song.”
answers: marriages [or weddings] (The first line is “Sonnet. To Generall Goring” and the second line is “Prothalamion” by Edmund Spenser.)
answers_sanitized: roses [accept "To Lucasta. The Rose"; accept "A Red, Red Rose"]
marriages [or weddings] (The first line is "Sonnet. To Generall Goring" and the second line is "Prothalamion" by Edmund Spenser.)

The Bishops' Wars also inspired Lovelace to write a sonnet to Goring about one of these events. Another poem written for one of these events repeats the line "Sweet Thames, run softly, till I end my song."

parts: The novel’s villain and Clarissa’s rapist has this last name, which he shares with the Cavalier poet of “To Althea, from Prison.”
Lovelace’s last words, “Let this expiate!” follow one of these events, which ends the novel. In a Henry Fielding novel, Mr. Fitzpatrick is stabbed during one of these events.
Following Clarissa’s rape, her letters take the form of the “mad papers,” the last of which is this paper. This paper contains quotes from different English poets scattered about the page.

parts: Name this poem that describes “flowing Cups… With no allaying Thames (“tems”).” This poem considers “The Gods that wanton in the Air” and “Enlargèd Winds, that curl the Flood” before concluding “Angels alone that soar above / Enjoy such Liberty.”

Richard Lovelace’s “To Amarantha, That She Would Dishevel Her Hair” and Robert Herrick’s “To The Virgins, To Make Much of Time” are examples of poems described by this phrase. This two-word phrase, which is Latin for “seize the day,” describes a common theme of Cavalier poetry.

bonuses.leadin: In Act 5 of Henry IV, Part One, Falstaff claims that this concept cannot “take away the grief of a wound” before calling it “a mere scutcheon.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this concept. Thomas Carew [“carey”] compared it to a giant that “keeps cowards out” in his poem “A Rapture,” which ends by asking why this “goblin” concept, “which the world adores, should make men atheists, and not women whores?”
This Cavalier poet declared “I could not love thee (Dear) so much / Lov’d I not honour more” at the end of his poem “To Lucasta, Going to the Wars.”

This crafty Shakespearean character asserts that “honour travels in a strait so narrow” in a speech beginning “Time hath, my lord, a wallet at his back.” In Act 1, Scene 3, he delivers a speech about the importance of social order which claims “Take but degree away, untune that string / And, hark, what discord follows!”

bonuses.leadin: This poet was arrested and imprisoned in Wormwood Scrubs due to his conscientious objection to the First World War. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this British poet who befriended and maintained a lifelong correspondence with Louis Zukofsky after a trip to New York. The lines “Brag, sweet tenor bull, / descant on Rawthey’s madrigal, / each pebble its part / for the fells’ late spring” open his best known work, Briggflatts.
This other poet wrote the line “Stone Walls do not a Prison make, / Nor Iron bars a Cage.”
While in Reading Gaol, [“jail”] this author wrote De Profundis to his lover Lord Alfred Douglas.
answers: Richard Lovelace [pronounced like the world loveless, rather than love-lace]
answers_sanitized: Richard Lovelace [pronounced like the world loveless, rather than love-lace]

This other poet wrote the line "Stone Walls do not a Prison make, / Nor Iron bars a Cage."
While in Reading Gaol, ["jail"] this author wrote De Profundis to his lover Lord Alfred Douglas.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the involvement of poets in the English Civil War, for 10 points each.
parts: This cavalier poet of “To Lucasta, Going to the Wars” wrote “To Althea, from Prison” while imprisoned during the first phase of the war.
While tutoring the daughter of Parliamentary general Thomas Fairfax, Andrew Marvell described this home of his employer as a “Work of no foreign architect” in a country house poem titled for it.

A few years before being implicated in the First Army Plot, this poet compared a bride’s feet to “little mice” and referenced having sex “With Bridget, and with Nell” in the poem “A Ballad upon a Wedding.”
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about the involvement of poets in the English Civil War, for 10 points each.

A few years before being implicated in the First Army Plot, this poet compared a bride's feet to "little mice" and referenced having sex "With Bridget, and with Nell" in the poem "A Ballad upon a Wedding."

bonuses.leadin: In the second stanza of this poem, the speaker resolves to “with a stronger faith embrace A sword, a horse, a shield.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 1649 poem, that ends with the speaker claiming “I could not love thee, dear, so much, Loved I not honor more.”
“To Lucasta, Going to the Warres” was written by this Cavalier poet, who wrote “Stone Walls do not a Prison make, Nor Iron bars a Cage” in “To Althea, from Prison.”

This other Cavalier poet included the line “’Twas I that gave thee thy renown” in the poem “Know, Celia, Since Thou Art So Proud.” Celia appears again in his best known poem, the 166-line “A Rapture.”

parts: Name this woman who is the addressee of a poem which proclaims “I could not love thee, dear, so much, loved I not honor more,” narrated by a soldier going off to war.

parts: Name this poem, whose speaker references Charles I when describing the “sweetness, mercy, majesty / And glories of my king,” before following up its most iconic line with “Minds innocent and quite take / That for an hermitage.”
“To Althea, From Prison” was written by this Cavalier poet, who defended his participation in the English Civil War in some other poems addressed to “Lucasta.”

Lovelace makes the poor East Wind into a pervy rapist of the “spicy nest” in a poem beseeching Amarantha to perform this action, a not-so-subtle stand-in for getting down with the speaker, who notes that “joys so ripe, so little keep.”
answers: Dishevel her hair [accept reasonable equivalents like unbraid or let down; anything involving messing up hair here is fine]
